title: List Newest Source Files Before Backup
hook: Before trusting a backup, know which files changed most recently.
problem: You need a timestamp-sorted view of source files before comparing backups.
starting_state: A source directory contains app, content, and asset files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads file timestamps and paths.
when_to_use: Use before backup checks or when you need to understand recent source changes.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as proof that backup contents match; pair it with checksums or rsync dry runs.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted list of source files with modification timestamps.

shorts.title: See newest source files.
shorts.voiceover: Before checking a backup, print source file timestamps so recent changes stand out.
shorts.caption: Start backup checks with file age.

linkedin.question: Do you check recent source changes before validating a backup?
linkedin.cta: Timestamp the source tree before comparing it.

youtube.description: Use find -printf to list source files with timestamps before backup checks.

title: Create a SHA256 Checksum Manifest
hook: A file list says what exists; checksums say whether bytes match.
problem: You need checksums for key files before copying or archiving them.
starting_state: A source directory contains a small set of important files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command calculates SHA256 hashes and prints them.
when_to_use: Use before a backup, migration, or manual copy when byte-level verification matters.
when_not_to_use: Do not paste checksum output containing sensitive filenames into public logs without review.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: SHA256 hashes followed by file paths.

shorts.title: Checksum important files.
shorts.voiceover: When bytes matter, create SHA256 hashes for the files you plan to back up or move.
shorts.caption: Checksums verify bytes, not just names.

linkedin.question: Do you generate checksums before moving critical small files?
linkedin.cta: Use checksums when exact bytes matter.

youtube.description: Generate SHA256 checksums for important source files.

title: Verify a SHA256 Checksum Manifest
hook: A checksum file is only useful if you actually verify it.
problem: You need to confirm files still match a saved SHA256 manifest.
starting_state: A checksums.sha256 file exists beside the source directory.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads files and checks hashes against the manifest.
when_to_use: Use after copying, restoring, or unpacking files from a backup.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it if the checksum manifest came from an untrusted source.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: One OK line per verified file.

shorts.title: Verify checksum manifests.
shorts.voiceover: After a restore or copy, run sha256sum -c so every listed file proves it still matches.
shorts.caption: Do the verification step.

linkedin.question: Do you verify checksum manifests after restores, or just create them?
linkedin.cta: Run the manifest check after copying data.

youtube.description: Verify a SHA256 checksum manifest with sha256sum -c.

title: Compare Source and Backup File Lists
hook: A backup can be missing files and still look plausible at a glance.
problem: You need to compare relative file paths in source and backup directories.
command: comm -3 <(find source -type f | sed 's#^source/##' | sort) <(find backup -type f | sed 's#^backup/##' | sort)
starting_state: Source and backup directories both contain files but may not match.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command compares sorted relative file lists.
when_to_use: Use when checking whether a backup contains the same files as the source tree.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to prove files have identical contents; compare checksums or use rsync itemization too.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Paths present only on one side of the comparison.

commands: comm -3 <(find source -type f | sed 's#^source/##' | sort) <(find backup -type f | sed 's#^backup/##' | sort)

shorts.title: Compare backup file lists.
shorts.voiceover: Strip the source and backup prefixes, sort both lists, then use comm to see what exists on only one side.
shorts.caption: File presence check before byte checks.

linkedin.question: Do you compare backup file presence separately from file contents?
linkedin.cta: Start with relative path lists, then verify bytes.

youtube.description: Use comm with find output to compare source and backup file lists.

title: Preview Backup Drift with rsync
hook: Rsync can tell you what would change before it changes anything.
problem: You need to see source-to-backup drift without modifying the backup.
starting_state: Source and backup directories have different files and content.
what_changed: Nothing changes because rsync runs with dry-run and itemized output.
when_to_use: Use before syncing backups, especially when --delete may remove stale files.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove -n until you understand every itemized change.
undo: No undo needed because this command is a dry run.
expected_output: Itemized rsync changes that would update, create, or delete files.

shorts.title: Preview rsync backup drift.
shorts.voiceover: Use rsync dry-run with itemized output before syncing backups, especially when delete is involved.
shorts.caption: Dry-run rsync before changing backups.

linkedin.question: Do you read rsync itemized output before removing --dry-run?
linkedin.cta: Keep -n until the drift makes sense.

youtube.description: Use rsync -ain --delete to preview source-to-backup drift safely.

title: Find Empty Files in a Backup
hook: Zero-byte files can be normal, or they can be failed writes.
problem: You need to spot empty files inside a backup tree.
starting_state: A backup directory contains regular files and temporary cache files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints zero-byte regular files.
when_to_use: Use after backup jobs, exports, or uploads where empty files may indicate failed writes.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every empty file is bad; lock files and placeholders can be intentional.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Paths to empty files in the backup directory.

shorts.title: Find zero-byte backup files.
shorts.voiceover: Search backups for zero-byte files after exports or uploads, then decide whether each one is expected.
shorts.caption: Empty files deserve a second look.

linkedin.question: Do you flag zero-byte files in backup reports?
linkedin.cta: Treat empty files as a triage signal, not automatic failure.

youtube.description: Find zero-byte files inside a backup directory with find.

title: List Largest Files in a Backup
hook: Large backup files are where storage surprises usually start.
problem: You need to rank backup files by size.
starting_state: A backup directory contains current files, stale files, and temporary files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints file sizes and paths sorted largest first.
when_to_use: Use when backups are growing or a storage bill suddenly changes.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete files just because they are large; confirm age, ownership, and restore value first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A largest-first list of backup files with byte counts.

shorts.title: Rank backup files by size.
shorts.voiceover: When backups grow unexpectedly, sort files by bytes before guessing what changed.
shorts.caption: Find the biggest backup files first.

linkedin.question: When backup storage grows, do you rank files by size first?
linkedin.cta: Use size ranking before cleanup decisions.

youtube.description: List backup files by size with find, sort, and head.

title: List Contents of a Backup Tarball
hook: You can inspect an archive without extracting it.
problem: You need to see what a backup tarball contains before restoring it.
starting_state: A tar archive exists in the archives directory.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists archive member names.
when_to_use: Use before extracting a backup archive or when checking whether expected files are present.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat a file listing as content verification; pair it with checksums when possible.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted sample of paths stored inside the tar archive.

shorts.title: Inspect tar before restore.
shorts.voiceover: Use tar -tf to list archive contents before extracting or restoring anything.
shorts.caption: List archive contents safely.

linkedin.question: Do you inspect tarball contents before extracting backups?

youtube.description: List tar archive contents without extracting a backup.

title: Count Source Files by Extension
hook: A quick extension count can show whether expected content made it into the source tree.
problem: You need a small inventory of source file types.
command: find source -type f -printf '%f\n' | sed -n 's/.*\.//p' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr
starting_state: A source directory contains config, markdown, and asset files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command derives extensions from filenames and counts them.
when_to_use: Use during backup sanity checks when a content type may be missing.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it for extensionless files unless you adapt the command.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: File extension counts sorted from most common to least common.

commands: find source -type f -printf '%f\n' | sed -n 's/.*\.//p' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr

shorts.title: Count file types fast.
shorts.voiceover: Count source file extensions to catch missing content classes before or after a backup.
shorts.caption: Simple inventory by extension.

linkedin.question: Do you inventory file types when validating backups?
linkedin.cta: Use extension counts as a quick sanity check.

youtube.description: Count source files by extension using find, sed, sort, and uniq.

title: Find Files Newer Than a Backup Snapshot
hook: Files newer than the last snapshot are the ones most likely missing from it.
problem: You need to list source files changed after the backup snapshot marker.
starting_state: A backup directory has a .snapshot marker file representing the last backup time.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command compares source file mtimes to the snapshot marker.
when_to_use: Use when checking what changed since the last backup ran.
when_not_to_use: Do not use timestamp checks as the only integrity signal; clock skew and preserved mtimes can mislead.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Source files newer than the backup snapshot marker.

shorts.title: What changed after backup?
shorts.voiceover: Compare source file times against a snapshot marker to find files newer than the last backup.
shorts.caption: Timestamp checks show likely backup gaps.

linkedin.question: Do you keep a snapshot marker for quick backup freshness checks?
linkedin.cta: Use newer-than checks as one backup signal.

youtube.description: Use find -newer to locate files changed after a backup snapshot marker.
